solution: Check NMI pod logs; refer to Microsoft Entra pod identity troubleshooting guide; verify pod identity binding and AzureIdentity resources

symptom: Key Vault Secrets Provider: keyvault.BaseClient#GetSecret context deadline exceeded
rootCause: Provider pod cannot access Key Vault due to firewall rules, network policies blocking egress, or network jitter on host network
solution: Add provider pods to firewall allowlist; check network policies; verify node connectivity to Entra ID and Key Vault using test pod on host network with curl

symptom: Key Vault Secrets Provider: StatusCode=400, Identity not found when mounting secrets store volume
rootCause: Incorrect userAssignedIdentityID specified in SecretProviderClass custom resource
solution: Get correct identity: az aks show --query addonProfiles.azureKeyvaultSecretsProvider.identity.clientId -o tsv; update SecretProviderClass

symptom: Key Vault Secrets Provider: StatusCode=403 Forbidden - Public network access is disabled and request is not from trusted service nor via approved private link
rootCause: AKS nodes and Key Vault private endpoint are on different virtual networks; no VNet link or peering configured
solution: Create virtual network link for AKS VNet at private DNS zone (privatelink.vaultcore.azure.net); add VNet peering between AKS and Key Vault VNets; verify with nslookup and nc

symptom: Key Vault Secrets Provider: driver name secrets-store.csi.k8s.io not found in list of registered CSI drivers
rootCause: Secrets Store CSI Driver pods not running on the node where the application pod is scheduled
solution: Check CSI Driver pod status: kubectl get pod -l app=secrets-store-csi-driver -n kube-system -o wide; check pod logs if not Running

symptom: Key Vault Secrets Provider: SecretProviderClass not found when mounting volume
rootCause: SecretProviderClass does not exist in same namespace as application pod or wrong name referenced in volume spec
solution: Create SecretProviderClass in same namespace as application pod; verify volumeAttributes.secretProviderClass name matches exactly

symptom: KEDA add-on fails to start: Failed to get API Group-Resources, EOF error connecting to API server; unable to run external metrics adapter
rootCause: Cluster firewall misconfigured, blocking KEDA from reaching Kubernetes API server
solution: Configure firewall to meet Azure Global required network rules for AKS egress traffic; verify KEDA pods running in kube-system

symptom: KEDA operator pods missing workload identity env vars (AZURE_TENANT_ID, AZURE_FEDERATED_TOKEN_FILE); cannot authenticate to Azure scalers
rootCause: KEDA add-on enabled before Workload Identity was configured; env vars not injected into operator pods
solution: Restart KEDA operator: kubectl rollout restart deployment keda-operator -n kube-system; verify env vars via kubectl describe pod

symptom: Managed namespace: FeatureNotFound when registering ManagedNamespacePreview; or BadRequest requiring feature flag
rootCause: ManagedNamespacePreview feature flag not registered or command entered incorrectly
solution: Register: az feature register --namespace Microsoft.containerService -n ManagedNamespacePreview; verify: az feature show

symptom: Managed namespace: cannot use system namespace names; cannot modify via kubectl - resource managed by ARM
rootCause: Reserved system namespace names blocked; managed namespaces controlled by ARM restrict kubectl modifications
solution: Use non-reserved namespace names; manage through ARM API, Azure portal, or az aks namespace CLI commands instead of kubectl

symptom: Network isolated AKS cluster: image pull fails due to network isolation; cannot pull from ACR
rootCause: ACR cache rules or private endpoint not properly configured; for managed ACR, bootstrap resources may be missing
solution: BYO ACR: verify cache rules and private endpoints. Managed ACR: check bootstrap resources, reconcile cluster if missing. Non-MCR images: create extra cache rules in private ACR

symptom: Network isolated cluster: image pull fails after updating cluster to network isolated or updating private ACR resource ID
rootCause: Kubelet configuration in CSE not updated after cluster update
solution: Reimage the node to update kubelet configuration following the Update ACR ID guide

symptom: Network isolated cluster: managed ACR, cache rules, private endpoints, or private DNS zones accidentally deleted
rootCause: Managed ACR infrastructure resources deleted
solution: Cache rule deleted: delete ACR then reconcile cluster. ACR/endpoints/DNS deleted: reconcile cluster

symptom: NAP/Karpenter nodes not removed - underused or empty nodes remain in cluster longer than expected
rootCause: Pods without proper tolerations, DaemonSets preventing drain, PDBs not correctly set, do-not-disrupt annotations, or locks blocking changes
solution: Add proper tolerations to pods, review DaemonSet configs, adjust PDBs to allow disruption, remove do-not-disrupt annotations, review lock configs

symptom: NAP/Karpenter pod connectivity issues - pods cannot communicate with other pods or external services on Karpenter-provisioned nodes
rootCause: NSG rules blocking traffic, incorrect subnet config in AKSNodeClass, CNI plugin issues (wrong conflist, missing IPs in CNS), or DNS resolution problems
solution: Review NSG rules, verify subnet config in AKSNodeClass, restart CNI plugin pods, check CoreDNS config, verify CNI conflist files in /etc/cni/net.d/

symptom: NAP/Karpenter DNS service IP mismatch - pods cannot resolve DNS or kubelet fails to register with API server on Karpenter nodes
rootCause: --dns-service-ip in AKSNodeClass incorrect or not matching kube-dns ClusterIP; DNS IP not in service CIDR; firewall blocking port 53
solution: Verify --dns-service-ip matches kube-dns ClusterIP, ensure DNS IP within service CIDR, check NSG allows port 53 TCP/UDP, restart CoreDNS if errored

symptom: NAP/Karpenter spot VM unexpected node terminations when using spot instances
rootCause: Azure spot VM eviction due to capacity reclaim or price threshold; insufficient instance type diversity
solution: Use diverse instance types in NodePool CRD, implement proper PDBs, consider mixed spot/on-demand strategies, use workloads tolerant of preemption

symptom: NAP/Karpenter VM creation fails with quota exceeded errors
rootCause: Azure subscription quota exceeded; NodePool CRD limited to few VM sizes increasing quota hit probability
solution: Request quota increases via Azure portal; expand NodePool CRDs to include more VM sizes

symptom: AGIC connectivity issues - ingress has no IP address or HTTP timeout accessing services behind Application Gateway Ingress Controller
rootCause: Application Gateway in stopped state, AGIC misconfigured, backend address pools not matching pod IPs, or AGIC pod errors
solution: Verify app via port-forward first; check ingress events; inspect AGIC logs (kubectl logs -n kube-system -l=app=ingress-appgw); verify App Gateway operationalState=Running; compare backend pool IPs with endpoints; start App Gateway if stopped

symptom: Managed NGINX ingress controller returns HTTP 502/504 gateway errors or significant latency under load
rootCause: CPU exhaustion on NGINX ingress pods; HPA reached max replicas or no nodes available; resource limits misconfigured
solution: Monitor HPA (kubectl get hpa -n app-routing-system -w); check pending pods; configure NginxIngressController CRD scaling (maxReplicas, minReplicas, threshold); default 500m CPU request with no limits

symptom: 401 Unauthorized accessing AKS control plane via external identity providers - token audience/issuer mismatch
rootCause: JWT authenticator audience/issuer URL mismatch with token claims or OAuth misconfigured
solution: Verify iss/aud claims match JWT authenticator config; az aks jwtauthenticator update; ensure outbound HTTPS to IdP

symptom: Token validation failures - invalid CEL expressions in JWT authenticator claim mappings
rootCause: CEL expressions have syntax errors or return wrong types
solution: Test CEL expressions; username must return string, groups string array; include aks:jwt: prefix

symptom: AKS workloads exit with permission denied or function not implemented after configuring seccomp profile
rootCause: Seccomp profile blocks required syscalls (clone, io_uring, clock_settime)
solution: Use Inspektor Gadget audit_seccomp to identify blocked syscalls; change defaultAction to SCMP_ACT_LOG for diagnosis

symptom: PostgreSQL on Azure Files: could not change permissions of directory - Operation not permitted
rootCause: Azure Files uses CIFS/SMB - permissions cannot be changed after mount
solution: Use Azure Disk plugin instead; use subPath property

symptom: PVC Pending - Failed to create file share: 403 AuthorizationFailure with dynamic provisioning
rootCause: Storage account firewall blocks persistentvolume-controller; useDataPlaneAPI blocked by firewall
solution: Use static provisioning; or allow AKS VNET in storage account networking

symptom: Azure Disk mount fails - Disk cannot be attached because not in same zone as VM
rootCause: Azure Disk LRS default; disk and node in different availability zones
solution: Use node affinity with topology.disk.csi.azure.com/zone; or ZRS disks; or Azure Files

symptom: Azure Disk mount AuthorizationFailed - no authorization for Microsoft.Compute/disks/read
rootCause: AKS identity lacks permissions on disk in different resource group
solution: Assign Contributor role to AKS identity on disk scope

symptom: Multi-Attach error - Volume already used by pod on different node
rootCause: Azure Disk only supports ReadWriteOnce; can attach to one node only
solution: Ensure disk not mounted by multiple pods on different nodes; use Azure Files for ReadWriteMany

symptom: Azure Disk mount fails - UltraSSD_LRS requires additionalCapabilities.ultraSSDEnabled
rootCause: Ultra disk attached to node pool without ultra disk support
solution: Create node pool with --enable-ultra-ssd flag

symptom: Azure Disk mount fails - applyFSGroup failed input/output error with many files
rootCause: Kubernetes recursively changes ownership for all files when fsGroup set; slow for large volumes
solution: Set fsGroupChangePolicy: OnRootMismatch in securityContext

symptom: FailedScheduling - node(s) exceed max volume count
rootCause: Node VM size reached maximum disk capacity limit
solution: Add node pool with larger VM size; scale pool; delete unused disks

symptom: Azure File share mount error(2) No such file or directory - pod ContainerCreating
rootCause: No connectivity on port 445: file share missing, NSG blocks, firewall blocks, or FIPS node pool
solution: Verify file share exists; check port 445; check NSG; for FIPS use NFS StorageClass

symptom: Azure File share mount error(13) Permission denied - storage account key mismatch after rotation
rootCause: Key rotated but Kubernetes secret still has old key
solution: kubectl edit secret to update base64-encoded key; recreate pods

symptom: Azure File share mount error(13) - storage account Selected Networks missing AKS VNET
rootCause: Storage account restricted to selected networks but AKS VNET not whitelisted
solution: Add AKS VNET and subnet to storage account networking

symptom: Azure File share mount error(13) via private link - FQDN resolves to public IP instead of private
rootCause: Private DNS zone missing virtual network link for AKS VNET
solution: Create virtual network link for AKS VNET in privatelink.file.core.windows.net DNS zone

symptom: Azure File share mount fails - encryption incompatible with AKS < 1.25 kernel
rootCause: AKS < 1.25 kernel 5.4 only supports AES-128; Maximum security disables AES-128-GCM
solution: Use Maximum compatibility profile or enable AES-128-GCM; AKS >= 1.25 supports AES-256-GCM

symptom: Azure File share mount error(13) - security profile without NTLM v2 authentication
rootCause: SMB security profile requires NTLM v2 but disabled
solution: Enable NTLM v2 or use Maximum compatibility profile

symptom: Azure Disk mount fails with wrong fs type when setting uid/gid in mountOptions
rootCause: ext4/xfs do not support uid=x,gid=x mount options
solution: Use securityContext with runAsUser/fsGroup; or init container with chown

symptom: Azure File share mount fails with 'host is down' after storage account key rotation
rootCause: Storage account key changed but Kubernetes secret azurestorageaccountkey still references old key
solution: Update azurestorageaccountkey in Kubernetes secret with base64-encoded new key; redeploy pods

symptom: BlobFuse mount fails errno=404 - Unable to start blobfuse
rootCause: Blob container referenced by PV/PVC does not exist
solution: Verify blob container exists in storage account; ensure PV references correct container name

symptom: BlobFuse mount fails errno=403 - authentication or connectivity issues
rootCause: Kubernetes secret has wrong storage key (key rotation) or AKS VNET not whitelisted on storage account
solution: Verify/update Kubernetes secret key; add AKS VNET to storage account networking

symptom: BlobFuse/NFS 3.0 mount context deadline exceeded or volume ID already exists
rootCause: NSG blocks port 443 (BlobFuse) or ports 111/2049 (NFS); or virtual appliance/firewall blocks traffic
solution: Check NSG allows outbound to storage on required ports; add route bypassing virtual appliance

symptom: NFS 3.0 Blob mount access denied by server
rootCause: AKS VNET/subnet not added to storage account selected networks
solution: Add AKS VNET and subnet to storage account networking firewall rules

symptom: Slow Azure Disk attach/detach operations with in-tree driver kubernetes.io/azure-disk
rootCause: In-tree Azure Disk driver performs attach/detach sequentially; >10 ops on single VM or >3 on VMSS
solution: Migrate to CSI driver (file.csi.azure.com / disk.csi.azure.com)

symptom: BYOK CreateVMSSAgentPoolFailed - KeyVaultNotPurgeProtectionEnabled
rootCause: Key vault created without purge protection enabled
solution: Recreate key vault with --enable-purge-protection true

symptom: BYOK CreateVMSSAgentPoolFailed - KeyVaultAndDiskInDifferentRegions
rootCause: Key vault and disk encryption set are in different Azure regions
solution: Create key vault in same region as disk encryption set

symptom: BYOK disk encryption set permission revoked - must grant permissions to key vault
rootCause: Disk encryption set permission for key vault was deleted
solution: Re-run az keyvault set-policy to grant DiskEncryptionSet access to key vault

symptom: Istio CNI DaemonSet (azure-service-mesh-istio-cni-addon-node) pods not running on all cluster nodes - CNI plugin not installed on some nodes causing pod network setup failures
rootCause: Node taints prevent CNI DaemonSet pods from being scheduled. The DaemonSet lacks appropriate tolerations to match custom node taints.
solution: Check node taints with kubectl describe nodes. Verify DaemonSet tolerations match. Check CNI DaemonSet pod logs for installation or permission errors.

symptom: Istio egress gateway deployment and service not provisioned - resources fail to create in user-managed namespace
rootCause: Self-managed mutating or validating admission webhooks block Istio egress gateway resource provisioning. AKS admissions enforcer cannot prevent custom webhooks in user namespaces.
solution: Check for custom admission webhooks blocking egress gateway resources. Verify egress gateway name is valid DNS name (<=63 chars, lowercase alphanumeric, hyphens, periods).

symptom: Istio outbound traffic to external services blocked - pods cannot reach external endpoints when outboundTrafficPolicy is REGISTRY_ONLY
rootCause: outboundTrafficPolicy.mode set to REGISTRY_ONLY requires explicit ServiceEntry for each external service. Egress gateways require ServiceEntry resolution set to DNS.
solution: Create ServiceEntry for each external service with spec.resolution DNS for egress gateways. Use exportTo to restrict scope. For HTTP proxy clusters, configure ServiceEntry per Azure HTTP proxy support docs.

symptom: Istio ingress gateway not receiving traffic despite correct firewall/NSG rules for user node pool subnets
rootCause: Ingress gateway pods scheduled on system node pools whose subnets are not included in firewall/NSG allow rules.
solution: Allow traffic to system node pool subnets in firewall/NSG rules. Add DNAT rule for Azure Firewall. Consider changing externalTrafficPolicy to Local.

symptom: Managed Gateway API CRD installation fails or conflicts with pre-existing self-managed Gateway API CRDs
rootCause: Pre-existing CRDs have incompatible bundle version, include experimental channel CRDs, or lack required annotations.
solution: Verify CRD bundle version matches Kubernetes version. Ensure only standard channel CRDs. Disable Managed Gateway API, uninstall self-managed CRDs, re-enable. Check app.kubernetes.io/managed-by: aks annotation.

symptom: Managed Gateway API CRD bundle version not updated after AKS cluster minor version upgrade
rootCause: After AKS minor version upgrade, CRDs should auto-upgrade to new supported bundle version but may fail silently.
solution: Verify CRD bundle version via gateway.networking.k8s.io/bundle-version annotation. If not updated, uninstall and reinstall Managed Gateway CRDs.

symptom: Istio Gateway API ingress routing fails - HTTPRoute or Gateway cross-namespace references broken
rootCause: Gateway allowedRoutes not configured for cross-namespace routing, or Route parentRefs referencing wrong Gateway namespace. gatewayClassName must be istio.
solution: Set gatewayClassName to istio. Configure allowedRoutes for cross-namespace. Ensure parentRefs reference correct Gateway name and namespace. Inspect Gateway status and istiod logs.

symptom: Istio Gateway API gateway pods not updated to new proxy image after minor revision canary upgrade
rootCause: During canary upgrade with two control planes, higher revision takes ownership of unlabeled Gateway resources. Explicitly labeled gateways must be relabeled.
solution: Verify gateway pods have new proxy image. Restart deployment if not updated. If gateways have explicit istio.io/rev labels, relabel to new revision before completing upgrade.

symptom: AKS cluster nodes cannot reach external identity provider - DNS resolution or network connectivity failures prevent JWT token validation
rootCause: Network security groups, Azure Firewall rules, or DNS misconfiguration block outbound HTTPS (port 443) traffic from cluster nodes to the identity provider domain (e.g. token.actions.githubusercontent.com, accounts.google.com).
solution: Verify DNS resolution from cluster node via kubectl debug. Check NSG rules allow outbound HTTPS to IdP domain. If using Azure Firewall, add application rules for IdP URLs. Test connectivity with curl to IdP .well-known/openid-configuration endpoint.

symptom: JWT authenticator rejects tokens because username or groups claims do not include required aks:jwt: prefix
rootCause: Claim mappings in JWT authenticator configuration do not prepend the mandatory aks:jwt: prefix to username and group claims, causing validation rule failures.
solution: Update JWT authenticator claimMappings to include aks:jwt: prefix in username expression (e.g. 'aks:jwt:github:' + claims.sub). For groups, use map(g, 'aks:jwt:' + g). Run az aks jwtauthenticator update with corrected config file.

symptom: Pods stuck in Terminating state in AKS cluster - kubectl delete pod does not complete
rootCause: Pod finalizers, dependent resources not cleaned up, or graceful shutdown period not expiring. Force delete may be required.
solution: Run kubectl delete pod <name> -n <ns> --grace-period=0 --force --wait=false. If still stuck, check for finalizers on the pod. Delete dependent resources first, then retry pod deletion.

symptom: Namespace stuck in Terminating state - cannot delete namespace even after removing all resources within it
rootCause: Namespace has finalizers that prevent garbage collection. Even after all resources are deleted, the finalizer keys remain on the namespace metadata.
solution: Patch namespace to remove finalizers: kubectl patch namespace <name> --patch '{"metadata": {"finalizers": null}}'. Then retry kubectl delete namespace. Also ensure all resources within namespace are deleted first using kubectl get all -n <ns>.

symptom: AKS pod workloads restart repeatedly: pods in CrashLoopBackOff or Error state, RESTARTS count increasing, liveness/readiness probe failure events in kubectl describe pod
rootCause: Liveness or startup probes misconfigured — timeout/failureThreshold/initialDelaySeconds do not account for actual startup time; missing startup probes for slow-initializing apps; probe endpoints depend on external resources (DB, storage) that may be temporarily unavailable; CPU throttling causes probe requests to timeout; health check logic is overly strict (full dependency validation in liveness probe instead of basic process check)
solution: 1) Add startup probes for slow-init apps with sufficient failureThreshold * periodSeconds; 2) Adjust liveness probe timeoutSeconds and failureThreshold to tolerate transient latency; 3) Keep liveness probes lightweight — only check process health, not dependencies; 4) Move dependency validation to readiness probes; 5) Ensure container CPU/memory limits are adequate for responding to probes under load

symptom: AKS applications experience intermittent timeouts or slow access to Azure Storage; pod logs contain Connection timeout, Connection reset by peer, or 503 Service Unavailable; kubectl describe pod shows no PVC errors (network-related issue)
rootCause: SNAT port exhaustion on AKS nodes using outbound connectivity via Azure Load Balancer under heavy traffic; public endpoint dependency makes traffic susceptible to transient network problems and DDoS protection throttling
solution: 1) Enable private endpoints for the storage account in same VNet as AKS cluster and ensure DNS resolves to private IP; 2) Assign NAT Gateway to AKS subnet for increased SNAT port capacity and stable outbound IP; 3) Implement application-level connection pooling to avoid excessive short-lived outbound connections; 4) Enable NSG flow logs and DDoS diagnostic logging to capture connection drops and anomalies

symptom: Pods remain in Pending state; kubectl describe pod shows event "pod didn't trigger scale-up (it wouldn't fit if a new node is added)"; cluster autoscaler does not scale up node count
rootCause: Pod resource requests (CPU/memory/GPU) exceed maximum resources available on any node in the cluster; node is missing a required resource (e.g. GPU); pod has affinity or topology constraints that new nodes cannot satisfy
solution: Review pod resource request configuration and compare with node size. Adjust node size/type or VM SKU to accommodate pod requests. Adjust pod resource requests if over-provisioned. Check for affinity rules or taints preventing scheduling and update them as needed.

symptom: Pod stuck in CrashLoopBackOff status on AKS cluster; pod repeatedly crashes and restarts with non-zero exit code
rootCause: Multiple possible causes: application failure (misconfig/missing dependencies/wrong env vars), resource limits too low causing OOMKill, missing or misconfigured ConfigMaps/Secrets, corrupted or wrong image tag, init container failures, misconfigured liveness/readiness probes, application dependencies not ready, networking issues, invalid ENTRYPOINT/command/arguments
solution: 1) kubectl describe pod <pod> to check events and status; 2) kubectl logs <pod> to examine application logs; 3) kubectl get pod <pod> -o yaml to read termination message; 4) Check resource limits vs actual usage; 5) Verify ConfigMaps/Secrets exist and are correctly mounted; 6) Validate container image and tag; 7) Check init container logs; 8) Review probe configuration

symptom: Managed NGINX ingress controller returns HTTP 502/504 gateway errors or significant response time increase compared to direct service access; indicates NGINX CPU exhaustion under load
rootCause: NGINX ingress controller pods experiencing CPU exhaustion during load spikes. HPA may have reached max replicas or no nodes available to schedule new NGINX pods. Resource limits/requests misconfiguration causing premature scaling.
solution: 1) Check HPA behavior: kubectl get hpa -n app-routing-system -w; 2) If HPA at max replicas, increase maxReplicas via NginxIngressController CRD spec.scaling.maxReplicas; 3) Check for pending pods: kubectl get pod --field-selector=status.phase=Pending -n app-routing-system; 4) Verify NGINX deployment resource limits: kubectl describe deploy nginx -n app-routing-system; 5) Configure CRD scaling: threshold (rapid/steady/balanced), minReplicas, maxReplicas

symptom: After upgrading AKS cluster to Kubernetes 1.25+, pods report increased memory usage; kubectl top node shows higher memory than before; increased pod evictions and memory pressure on nodes
rootCause: Kubernetes 1.25 on AKS defaults to cgroup v2 which uses different memory accounting — kernel memory (page cache, slab, etc.) is now included in container memory stats, making reported usage appear higher
solution: If frequent memory pressure: upgrade VM size for more memory. If higher pod eviction rate: increase memory limits and requests for pods. Update apps to cgroup v2-compatible versions (OpenJDK jdk8u372+/11.0.16+/15+, uber-go/automaxprocs v1.5.1+). Temporary workaround: revert to cgroup v1 using the AKS revert-cgroup-v1 DaemonSet (test in non-prod first)

symptom: Pod is stuck in CrashLoopBackOff status on AKS cluster; container repeatedly crashes and restarts with non-zero exit code
rootCause: Multiple possible causes: application failure (misconfig, missing dependencies, wrong env vars), resource limits too low (OOMKill), missing/misconfigured ConfigMaps or Secrets, corrupted or wrong-tag image, init container failures, misconfigured liveness/readiness probes, application dependencies not ready, networking issues, invalid ENTRYPOINT/command/arguments
solution: Use kubectl describe pod to check events; kubectl logs to examine container output; kubectl get pod --output=yaml to read termination message; check resource requests/limits; verify ConfigMaps/Secrets exist and are correct; validate image tag and registry access; review probe configuration; check init container logs; verify dependent services are available

symptom: Managed NGINX ingress controller returns HTTP 502/504 gateway errors or significant response time difference between service and end-to-end latency during load spikes
rootCause: CPU exhaustion on NGINX ingress controller pods; HPA may have reached max replicas or no nodes available to schedule additional NGINX pods; resource limits/requests misconfiguration causing premature scaling
solution: Check HPA behavior: kubectl get hpa -n app-routing-system -w; verify NGINX pods are not in Pending state; check deployment resource limits via kubectl describe deploy nginx -n app-routing-system; configure NginxIngressController CRD scaling (maxReplicas, minReplicas, threshold) to allow more aggressive scaling; do not modify deployment limits directly, use the CRD instead

symptom: AKS node VM enters failed state after Azure Disk detach operation partially fails. Node becomes unusable.
rootCause: A partial failure during Azure Disk detach leaves the node VM in a failed provisioning state.
solution: For availability-set-based clusters: run "az vm update --resource-group <rg> --name <vm>". For VMSS-based clusters: run "az vmss update-instances --resource-group <rg> --name <scale-set> --instance-id <id>" to manually update the VM status.

symptom: AKS cluster enters failed state after creation or upgrade. Activity log shows errors like OutboundConnFailVMExtensionError, drain error, SubscriptionNotRegistered, RequestDisallowedByPolicy, QuotaExceeded, or PublicIPCountLimitReached.
rootCause: Multiple possible causes: VM extension provisioning failure due to outbound connectivity issues, pod drain failures, unregistered subscription provider, Azure Policy blocking operations, resource quota exceeded, or public IP limit reached.
solution: Check activity log for specific error code. For OutboundConnFail: verify NSG/firewall allows outbound. For drain error: force delete stuck pods. For SubscriptionNotRegistered: run "az provider register --namespace Microsoft.ContainerService". For QuotaExceeded: request quota increase. For PublicIPCountLimitReached: delete unused public IPs or request limit increase. Then reconcile cluster with "az resource update --ids <cluster-id>".

symptom: AKS node pool enters failed state. VMSS provisioning state shows Failed. Common errors: insufficient capacity, quota limits, policy violations, resource locks.
rootCause: VM scale set backing the node pool encounters provisioning/scaling/updating error due to capacity, quota, network, policy, or resource lock issues.
solution: Check node pool status: "az aks nodepool show". Check VMSS: "az vmss show". Check VM instances: "az vmss list-instances". Review activity log and diagnostic settings. Verify quota with "az vm list-usage". Check and adjust policies with "az policy". Check and remove resource locks with "az lock". If needed, delete and recreate the node pool.

symptom: Slow container image pulls in AKS clusters running versions earlier than 1.31. Large or numerous images take excessive time to pull, causing delayed pod startup.
rootCause: AKS versions before 1.31 use serialized image pulls by default, pulling images one at a time which is less performant.
solution: Upgrade AKS cluster to version 1.31 or later to automatically switch to parallel image pulls. Parallel pulls generally improve overall image pull performance.

symptom: After upgrading to AKS 1.31+, parallel image pulls cause increased latency or disk throttling compared to serialized pulls. First image pull slower, disk bandwidth saturated.
rootCause: Parallel image pulls can throttle disk I/O, especially with unoptimized disk and VM resources. Multiple simultaneous pulls saturate disk bandwidth.
solution: Use ephemeral OS disks instead of managed OS disks. Increase OS disk size for more bandwidth. Use newer VM SKUs (V4/V5 series) for better storage/network throughput. Increase VM SKU size for more CPU cores. If needed, disable parallel pulls: "az feature register --namespace Microsoft.ContainerService --name DisableParallelImagePulls".

symptom: Pod stuck in Pending state with scheduler error: 0/(X) nodes are available: Y node(s) had volume node affinity conflict. Persistent Volume nodeAffinity rules do not match any available node labels.
rootCause: Persistent Volumes define nodeAffinity rules restricting which nodes can access the volume. If no available nodes satisfy the PV affinity rules, the scheduler cannot assign the pod.
solution: Check PV nodeAffinity with kubectl get pv -o yaml. Verify node labels match PV affinity rules (kubectl get nodes --show-labels). Either update PV nodeAffinity to match existing nodes, or add required labels to nodes: kubectl label nodes <node-name> <key>=<value>.

symptom: Pod workloads in AKS restart repeatedly, showing CrashLoopBackOff or Error status. RESTARTS count increases over time. kubectl describe pod shows liveness/readiness probe failures.
rootCause: Liveness or startup probes are misconfigured: timeout, failureThreshold, or initialDelaySeconds do not account for application startup time. Missing startup probes for slow-initializing apps cause liveness probe to run before app is ready. Probe endpoints depending on external resources fail even when app is healthy.
solution: Configure startup probes for slow-initializing apps with adequate failureThreshold * periodSeconds. Adjust liveness probe timeoutSeconds and failureThreshold to tolerate transient latency. Keep liveness probes lightweight (basic process health only). Move dependency checks to readiness probes. Ensure CPU/memory limits allow app to respond to probes.

symptom: AKS node that was previously healthy changes to NotReady status. kubelet stops posting Ready status. Connections stay idle for long time causing SNAT port exhaustion.
rootCause: SNAT port exhaustion on the node: idle connections rely on default 30-min timeout, consuming SNAT ports. Also caused by IOPS performance problems, threading issues (OOM on thread creation), or network-level changes (DNS, firewall, NSG, route table).
solution: Check for SNAT issues: reduce idle connection timeout, review outbound connectivity patterns, scale outbound public IPs and allocated outbound ports. For IOPS: deploy larger disk sizes or increase node SKU. For threading: check ulimit settings. For network: verify DNS, firewall rules, NSG, route tables. Stop and restart affected nodes.

symptom: On Windows AKS nodes, container log file sizes grow far beyond the container-log-max-size kubelet setting during aggressive log writing. Logs can grow to dozens of GiB before rotation occurs even if max size is set to dozens of MiB.
rootCause: During heavy log writing, the log file grows too quickly for kubelet log rotation to occur before the container-log-max-size limit is exceeded. This is a known Kubernetes issue affecting all K8s versions on Windows.
solution: No fix available at the Kubernetes level (open issue kubernetes/kubernetes#110630). Mitigate by reducing log verbosity in the application, using an external log aggregation pipeline (e.g., Fluentd sidecar), or setting larger disk sizes for the OS disk to absorb log spikes.

symptom: On Windows AKS nodes, kubelet log file compression fails - kubelet hangs during the final step when trying to rename the .gz archive before closing the file.
rootCause: Bug in Kubernetes log compression on Windows where the kubelet stops responding when renaming the compressed archive. Affects all K8s versions before 1.25.0, and specific 1.23.x (before 1.23.13) and 1.24.x (before 1.24.7) versions.
solution: Upgrade to Kubernetes 1.25.0 or later where the fix (kubernetes/kubernetes PR #111549) is included. For 1.24.x, upgrade to 1.24.7+. For 1.23.x, upgrade to 1.23.13+.

symptom: Custom OS configuration (sysctl settings) does not get applied on Windows node pools in AKS. The custom node configuration silently fails without error.
rootCause: Custom OS configurations are not supported on Windows node pools in AKS. Only Linux node pools support custom OS configuration. This is a platform limitation.
solution: Apply custom OS configuration only at the cluster level or node pool level for Linux node pools. Windows node pools do not support custom OS configuration. Verify settings are applied using: az aks show or kubectl describe node.

symptom: AKS cluster creation, scale-up, or upgrade fails with QuotaExceeded or InsufficientVCPUQuota error. Error message indicates exceeding approved vCPU quota limits (family-level like standardDSv3Family or regional-level Total Regional Cores).
rootCause: The operation exceeds the current vCPU quota limits for the subscription. This can be either a VM family-specific quota (e.g., standardDSv3Family) or a regional total cores quota.
solution: For family quota errors: increase VM-family vCPU quotas via Azure portal quota requests. For regional quota errors: select a different region or increase regional vCPU quota. File a Service and subscription limits (quotas) support ticket in Azure portal to request quota increase.
